name: Hayward's Quarry, Massachusetts - Braintree Member
short_description: Historic quarry site in the Braintree Member shale formation. Collect feldspar, quartz, and mica specimens from Ordovician-age sedimentary rocks near Boston.
description:
Hayward's Quarry is a well-known Massachusetts rockhounding destination located in the Braintree shale member, part of the Ordovician Ponkapoag Group. The site exposes fine-grained sedimentary rocks rich in feldspar and quartz minerals that formed in ancient marine environments over 450 million years ago. The relatively flat terrain and accessible parking make this an ideal beginner to intermediate site for collectors seeking quality mineral specimens.

The Braintree Member shale has been quarried historically for use as slate and building stone, which has left excellent exposures of the underlying rock layers. These fresh exposures reveal abundant feldspar crystals, particularly microcline and plagioclase varieties, along with accessory quartz and mica. The dark gray shale matrix contrasts nicely with the lighter mineral crystals, making specimens visually attractive.

Visitors can expect to find small to medium-sized hand specimens with well-formed crystal faces. The site's low elevation (120 feet) and flat topography mean minimal climbing and easy access for most ability levels. The quarry setting provides natural walls and overburden that protect collectors from weather while offering excellent mineral exposure.
history: The Braintree Member shale was deposited during the Ordovician Period in a shallow marine environment as the ancestral Atlantic Ocean covered New England. Subsequent compaction and burial transformed these sediments into fine-grained shale, with feldspar and quartz crystals forming during diagenesis. The site was actively quarried in the 19th and early 20th centuries for slate and building materials, creating the exposures rockhounds use today.
tips: Search the loose rubble piles and fresh fracture surfaces along the quarry walls where feldspar crystals are most visible against the dark shale matrix. Use a crack hammer and cold chisel to carefully extract specimens from the shale beds - strike parallel to the layering rather than perpendicular for best results. Look for glassy-white or pink feldspar faces, often clustered in groups of 5-20mm crystals; specimens with mica books attached are prized by collectors.
terrain_notes: Flat quarry floor at 120 feet elevation with minimal slope. Terrain consists of exposed shale walls, loose rubble piles, and level ground. Easy walking on most areas, but wear sturdy boots for broken rock fragments. No vegetation obstruction. Parking area approximately 50 feet from main collecting zone.
best_season: May - October. Spring through fall offers dry conditions ideal for quarry work. Winter frost can make rock surfaces slippery, and heavy rain may cause water pooling in the quarry floor.
written_directions: From Braintree town center, take Route 37 west for approximately 1.5 miles. Turn south on Pond Street and proceed 0.5 miles to the quarry access road on the right (east side). Park in the designated pullout area near the quarry entrance. The collecting area is immediately accessible from the parking zone.
road_conditions: Paved road to quarry entrance. Parking area is gravel with minor ruts. No high-clearance vehicle required.
parking_notes: Small gravel pullout accommodating 4-6 vehicles. Shaded by nearby trees. No formal facilities.
collection_rules: Public rockhounding site with no formal restrictions documented. Collect responsibly for personal use only. Do not damage or deface quarry walls beyond safe extraction methods. Remove only what you can safely extract. Be aware that some portions may be private property - respect boundary markers. Do not drive vehicles into the quarry floor.
rules:
Personal use collection only
Do not damage quarry walls unnecessarily
Remove loose material and rubble only
No motorized equipment
Respect private property boundaries if posted
nearest_services: Braintree town center, approximately 1.5 miles. Gas, food, and water available in downtown Braintree.
accessibility_notes: Flat terrain accessible to most hikers and rockhounders. Gravel parking area is level and easily reached from vehicles. Quarry floor is flat with no significant elevation change. Sturdy footwear recommended due to sharp rock fragments. No wheelchair access to quarry floor due to uneven terrain, but parking and road access are paved.

faq.question: What minerals can you find at Hayward's Quarry in Braintree, Massachusetts?
faq.answer: Hayward's Quarry is best known for feldspar specimens, particularly microcline and plagioclase varieties with crystal faces ranging from 5-20mm. You can also collect quartz crystals and mica books embedded in the dark Ordovician shale matrix. The contrast between light mineral crystals and dark host rock makes specimens visually striking.

faq.question: Is rockhounding free at Hayward's Quarry, and do I need a permit?
faq.answer: Yes, rockhounding at Hayward's Quarry is free and does not require a permit. It is a public site in Massachusetts with no documented access fees. However, always respect any posted private property boundaries and collect responsibly for personal use only.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound at Hayward's Quarry near Boston?
faq.answer: May through October offers the best collecting conditions. Spring and fall provide dry weather ideal for quarry work. Winter frost makes rock surfaces slippery, and heavy rain can cause water pooling that limits access to collecting areas.

faq.question: Is Hayward's Quarry suitable for beginner rockhounders and families with children?
faq.answer: Yes, Hayward's Quarry is excellent for beginners and families. The flat terrain at 120 feet elevation requires minimal hiking, and specimens are relatively easy to extract from loose rubble. Children ages 6 and up can participate safely when supervised and wearing sturdy footwear to avoid sharp rock fragments.

faq.question: What tools do I need to collect specimens at Hayward's Quarry in Massachusetts?
faq.answer: A crack hammer and cold chisel are ideal for carefully extracting feldspar and quartz specimens from the shale matrix. A small magnifying loupe helps identify crystal faces and quality. Gloves protect hands from sharp edges. Strike parallel to shale layers rather than perpendicular for best results and specimen quality.

faq.question: How far is Hayward's Quarry from Boston, and what are directions from Braintree?
faq.answer: Hayward's Quarry is located 1.5 miles southwest of Braintree town center, making it easily accessible from the Boston metro area. From downtown Braintree, take Route 37 west 1.5 miles, turn south on Pond Street for 0.5 miles, then turn right onto the quarry access road. Paved roads provide easy access with no high-clearance vehicle required.

meta_description: Find feldspar, quartz, and mica at Hayward's Quarry near Braintree, MA. Free public rockhounding on Ordovician shale. Beginner-friendly.
